# Discussion

## Google Timeline data

In the google timeline data, the accuracy is stated to be up to several hundred meters. Therefore the matching with roadtypes is inaccurate. An example of road type mismatch is the commuting from home to ZHAW and back in @fig-road_type_mismatch. From Rapperswil train station to Wädenswil train station, only the train was used. But in the road type matching, several points are not matched as 'Train' and sometimes even far away from the railroads.

![Example of mismatched road types on the way from home to ZHAW (only train from Rapperswil to Wädenswil).](plots/road_type_home_zhaw.png){#fig-road_type_mismatch}

Another attempt to evaluate the accuracy of the road type matching is to have a look at the from google stated activities at these points. As they include more than the coordinates of the points (acceleration etc.) in their analysis, it is estimated to be more accurate. The percentages of different road types within the different google activities is shown in @fig-road_type_activity.

![Percentage of different road types within the google matched activity.](plots/activity_road_type_amelia.png){#fig-road_type_activity width="85%"}

At a first glance, some of the road types seem to be matched very wrong, but some of them can somehow be explained. The 26% of walking on the rail might be the changing of trains at train stations, the tram line is inside of the main road and therefore 68% matched with it. Nevertheless, a lot of the other percentages (e.g. cycling on rail or train on local road) cannot be explained other than being errors.




## Processing efforts and accuracies

Cleaning and filtering the GPS data proved considerably more time-consuming than processing the Google Timeline data. Within buildings and tunnels, GPS measurements frequently deviated substantially from the actual position, and repeated app crashes introduced multiple gaps in the dataset. Furthermore, the high temporal resolution of the GPS data resulted in an extremely large number of recorded points, further increasing processing effort. In contrast, speed and travel distance calculations based on the Google Timeline data were less accurate, as the sampling interval ranged up to several minutes. Additionally, the positional accuracy of the Google Timeline data was generally lower than that of the GPS data.


### Discussion

The results show that both datasets can be used to describe general mobility patterns, but the reliability of the derived indicators depends strongly on the tracking method. Christopher’s GPS Logger data produced dense and spatially precise trajectories. This made the movement paths easier to interpret and improved the road type matching. In contrast, Amelia’s Google Timeline data contained fewer points and larger positional uncertainties. As a result, some movements were represented by straight connections between distant points, which can affect distance, speed and road type estimates.

A clear difference is visible in the road type results. For Christopher, rail accounts for the largest share of matched movement points, with about 56%. This fits the visible trajectories and indicates that train travel was an important part of his mobility. Amelia’s road type distribution is more balanced between local roads, main roads and rail. However, the comparison with Google activity labels shows several mismatches, such as walking or cycling points being assigned to rail. This suggests that the road type classification is less reliable for the Google Timeline data, especially where roads and railway lines are close to each other.

The movement indicators also show both similarities and differences between the two individuals. Average travel speed and commuting time to ZHAW are relatively similar, while Amelia shows higher variability in maximum radius, total distance and time away from home. Some of these differences probably reflect real differences in daily mobility behaviour, but they may also be influenced by the different data sources. Google Timeline can underestimate or simplify movements because of its lower temporal resolution, while the GPS Logger can produce many noisy points and requires more preprocessing.

Several limitations should therefore be considered. The analysis is based on only two individuals and a relatively short observation period, so the results cannot be generalized. In addition, the datasets were collected with different methods, sampling intervals and accuracy levels. GPS Logger data was precise under open sky, but gaps occurred due to app crashes, tunnels and poor indoor signal. Google Timeline was easier to process, but its lower resolution limited the accuracy of detailed spatial analyses.

Future improvements could include a longer tracking period, more participants and more advanced map-matching methods. For GPS Logger data, aggregating nearby points could reduce noise and processing time. For Google Timeline data, manually validating selected trips would help to assess how reliable the calculated indicators are. Overall, the project shows that personal tracking data is useful for exploring mobility patterns, but exact comparisons require careful preprocessing and cautious interpretation.
